coredata.dc:title: In situ growth of carbon quantum dots on acid/base 3D Co2VO4 nanoplates to regulate photocatalysis and peroxymonosulfate activation towards highly efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin

coredata.dc:description: Water contamination is considered as one of the greatest threats to the aquatic and human health. In this study, we focused on the fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) deposited Co2VO4 nano-hexagon sheets for visible light driven catalysis of ciprofloxacin (CIP) surging peroxymonosulphate (PMS) activation. The prepared nanocomposite (NCs) Co2VO4 base @CQDs (CVB/CQDs) achieved an exceptionally high CIP degradation (99.4 %) in presence of PMS, as well as the prepared counterparts Co2VO4 acid @CQDs (CVA/CQDs), Co2VO4 base, and Co2VO4 acid showed an appreciable photocatalytic performance against CIP. The rate of the reaction (k) of CVB/CQDs is 0.372 min−1 which was 3 times greater than CVB (0.105 min−1) and CVA/CQDs exhibit 0.119 min−1 which was 2.6 times greater than CVA (0.0451 min−1). The prepared NCs was characterized by using FE-SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, XPS, RAMAN, EIS, UV–vis DRS and PL techniques. From the scavenging and radical trapping experiments it was confirmed that the production of •OH and SO4 •− are key radicals which participated in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. The mechanism of degradation was proposed based on the results obtained from scavenging assays and DRS. The degradation pathway was predicted based on the GC–MS results and the intermediate's toxicity was analysed using ECOSAR program. The NCs showed exceptional stability and reusability after six consecutive cycles. This current study provides a new strategy for Co2VO4 based catalysis and PMS activation as a promising candidate in water remediation and removal of contaminants from water.

head.ce:title: In situ growth of carbon quantum dots on acid/base 3D Co2VO4 nanoplates to regulate photocatalysis and peroxymonosulfate activation towards highly efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water contamination is considered as one of the greatest threats to the aquatic and human health. In this study, we focused on the fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) deposited Co2VO4 nano-hexagon sheets for visible light driven catalysis of ciprofloxacin (CIP) surging peroxymonosulphate (PMS) activation. The prepared nanocomposite (NCs) Co2VO4 base @CQDs (CVB/CQDs) achieved an exceptionally high CIP degradation (99.4 %) in presence of PMS, as well as the prepared counterparts Co2VO4 acid @CQDs (CVA/CQDs), Co2VO4 base, and Co2VO4 acid showed an appreciable photocatalytic performance against CIP. The rate of the reaction (k) of CVB/CQDs is 0.372 min−1 which was 3 times greater than CVB (0.105 min−1) and CVA/CQDs exhibit 0.119 min−1 which was 2.6 times greater than CVA (0.0451 min−1). The prepared NCs was characterized by using FE-SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, XPS, RAMAN, EIS, UV–vis DRS and PL techniques. From the scavenging and radical trapping experiments it was confirmed that the production of •OH and SO4 •− are key radicals which participated in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. The mechanism of degradation was proposed based on the results obtained from scavenging assays and DRS. The degradation pathway was predicted based on the GC–MS results and the intermediate's toxicity was analysed using ECOSAR program. The NCs showed exceptional stability and reusability after six consecutive cycles. This current study provides a new strategy for Co2VO4 based catalysis and PMS activation as a promising candidate in water remediation and removal of contaminants from water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are generally used for the treatment against infectious diseases. The frequent usage of antibiotics leads to the distribution of the antibiotic residues in the environment, which subsequently facilitate the growth of the antibiotic resistance bacteria and antibiotic resistance genes. Furthermore, the growth of antibiotic-resistant traits affects the ecological system and human health [1]. These antibiotics enter the water bodies through various anthropogenic activities such as mishandled disposal of medical waste, various industrial waste disposal, and disposal of agricultural wastes. CIP is a fluoroquinolone group antibiotic that enters the water bodies which leads to stress formation in microorganisms that induces the production of anti-biotic resistance genes (ARGs) in microorganisms. Further, the accumulation of CIP in water bodies and other organisms leads to the disruption of human nervous system and endocrine system when it is consumed. Removing CIP from the water bodies through regular water purification technologies has always been a challenging task [2]. Conventional pollutant removal techniques like adsorption, catalysis, and microbial degradation have limitations such as incomplete degradation, high costs, and dependency on environmental conditions. Advanced Oxidation Techniques (AOTs) overcome these challenges by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) that break down pollutants into harmless byproducts with high efficiency [3]. Several conventional methods are reported for the removal of organic pollutants [4,5] and inorganic pollutants [6]. Importantly, microbial bioremediation [7,8] and catalytic oxidation techniques [9,10] were employed for the removal of pollutants. Guo et al. [11] reported the construction of a novel hierarchical tubular heterostructure COFs@MoS2-Pd with excellent catalytic performance. Semiconductor nanomaterial based photocatalytic technology has been proven to be an effective method for energy conversion and water treatment [12,13]. Photocatalysis is one of the important advanced oxidation processes employed for the removal of toxic organic pollutants [14]. Recent studies reported the enhanced activity by the activation of photocatalyst by using NaBH4 [15]. Hence, the present study we have used peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to activate the photocatalyst to enhance its performance. Coupling of Fenton process also could enhance the overall activity for the removal of pollutants [16].

Advanced oxidative process (AOP) is a method of water treatment technology that produce free radicals such as hydroxyl (•OH), superoxide radical (O2 •−) and sulfate radical (SO4 •−) to completely mineralize the organic pollutant present in the water [17,18]. Among all the AOPs, Fenton oxidation as a typical •OH radical - based AOP has created a great impact in water treatment [19]. However, fenton oxidation requires a huge amount of ferrite source and acidic condition for a better performance [20]. In the field of environmental remediation, sulfate radical based advanced oxidative process (SR-AOPs) dominates the traditional fenton oxidation [21]. Furthermore, the oxidation potential of sulfate radicals (SO4 •−/SO4 2−, E0 = 2.60–2.10 V) is greater than •OH radical (•OH/H2O, E0 = 1.90–2.70 V) [22]. Metal ions have been prominently used in the activation of PMS, the homogeneous catalyst Co2+ activates the PMS and generate SO4 •− which plays a major role in the photocatalytic process [23]. Considering all the transition metals, cobalt-based catalysts exhibit a higher standard oxidation/redox potential (1.92 V) which is a foremost parameter in the photocatalytic process [24]. Moreover, previous studies have proven that metallic cobalt has the ability to enhance the conductivity of the catalyst and promotes water dissociation by limiting the energy barrier for H* adsorption [25]. An ideal photocatalyst should be highly photoactive, nontoxic, physiologically and chemically inert, and economically practical [26]. The fact that, decoration of a nonmetal such as nitrogen, carbon, iodine, sulphur and fluorine, does lead to increase in the electron-hole recombination rate. Moreover, these nonmetals are more thermally stable and exhibit a considerable red shift towards the visible range in the electromagnetic spectrum [27]. Metavanadates (MV2O6) have emerged as promising photocatalysts due to their unique structural, optical, and electronic properties. These materials exhibit excellent photo-stability, chemical stability, and corrosion resistance, making them suitable for various photocatalytic applications [28]. Among the different metavanadates, ZnV2O6, NiV2O6, CuV2O6, MnV2O6, and CoV2O6 have attracted significant attention due to their tunable band gaps and efficient charge carrier dynamics [29]. The presence of vanadium in these compounds plays a crucial role in charge transfer and redox reactions, thereby enhancing their photocatalytic performance. Particularly, the unique electronic properties exhibited by Co2VO4 are due to the interaction between electrochemically transition metal ions cobalt and vanadate atoms. Further, recent studies have proven that metallic cobalt improves the conductivity of the catalyst which facilitates the photocatalytic property of the prepared catalyst [30]. Zhou and Jin [31] refers that Co2VO4 is a transition metal particle which shows an exceptional performance in photocatalytic activity. Further, it exhibits an enhanced electrochemical property. Co2VO4 is generally used in batteries, fuel cells, and photocatalytic hydrogen production. The combination of Co2VO4 with other transition metals and non-metals enhances photocatalytic activity of the NPs. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are commonly composed of amorphous to a nanocrystalline core carbon predominantly with sp2 hybridization. CQDs possess various properties such as enhanced absorption of visible light (sun light) and variable photoluminescence [32]. Further, recent research has proven that many of the photocatalyst uses limited amount of sunlight and has a high recombination rate which limits the overall efficiency of photocatalysis [33]. CQDs - deposition Co2VO4 NCs exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to bare Co2VO4 NPs which corresponds to its exceptional photoactivity and decreased electron-hole pair. This is due to its unique physical and chemical properties which displays an enhanced physicochemical property of the NCs. The enhanced catalytic properties of NCs depend upon its size, shape, chemical composition, oxidation states and the base NPs [34]. The structural and chemical characterization of the NCs is regarded as the most significant analysis as it defines all of these parameters as it influences the physicochemical properties of the catalyst [35]. The following characterizations such as FE-SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, XPS and Raman spectroscopy were used to analyse the chemicals composition, crystallinity, chemical state and surface properties of the catalyst [36]. SO4 •− based AOPs has drawn a lot of interest in waste water treatment technologies due to its high reactivity with organic contaminants and selectivity [37]. Furthermore, SO4 •− contains a high redox potential (2.5–3.1 V) which has been proven to be one of the strongest single-electron oxidants that participates in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [38].

The proposed study emphasizes on the construction of quantum dots deposited Co2VO4 NCs for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. The photoactivity of the NCs is enhanced by the addition of sulfate radicals using PMS. This approach holds a potential to effectively degrade pharmaceutical contaminants using visible-light contributing to sustainable water treatment technologies.

The chemicals employed in this study comprise cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Merck, India. Glucose (C6H12O6) was obtained from Loba, India. The analytical-grade quality of these high-purity chemicals guarantees the accuracy and reliability of the experimental outcomes.

The synthesis of Co2VO4 was performed as follows. Initially, cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2) and ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. In parallel, plant extract from L. delavayanum was prepared by boiling 10 g of its leaves in 100 mL of deionized water at 100 °C for 10 min. After cooling, the solution was filtered, and its final volume was adjusted to 100 mL.

Once the plant extract was ready, 10 mL of the extract was added to the precursor solution containing cobalt nitrate and ammonium metavanadate. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 9 using sodium hydroxide (NaOH). This solution was then transferred to a round-bottom flask, and reflux distillation was performed at 80 °C for 10 h. The resulting nanoparticles (NPs) - Co2VO4 base or CVB were collected, thoroughly washed with ethanol and deionized water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. To synthesize the acid-modified counterpart of the NPs, a slightly different procedure was followed. After adding the precursors and plant extract, 1 M HCl was introduced dropwise into the solution while stirring for 30 min. During this process, the solution changed colour from pale yellow to light brown. The mixture was then transferred to a round-bottom flask and subjected to reflux distillation at 80 °C for 10 h. Dark brown crystals formed at the bottom of the flask, indicating the successful synthesis of the acid-modified NPs (Co2VO4 acid or CVA). The resulting NPs were then collected, washed with ethanol and deionized water, and dried for 12 h in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

This procedure resulted in two variations of Co2VO4 NPs—base (CVB) and acid (CVA)—each synthesized under distinct pH conditions, providing materials with different properties for further analysis. Generally, plant extracts consist of various bioactive compounds and biochemicals that constitute to the antimicrobial, anticancer, antifungal, and anti-oxidant properties of the plant. At the same time these phytochemicals act as reducing and stabilizing agents. Additionally, the presence of flavonoids, polyphenols, and other secondary metabolites contributes to the enhanced photocatalytic activity by improving charge carrier separation and surface modification [39]. As a result, the plant phytochemicals enhance the reaction rate and adheres to the surface of the NPs resulting in an increased photocatalytic activity of the prepared NPs [40].

To synthesize the NCs, a solution of 1 g of glucose was prepared by dissolving it in 50 mL of distilled water, and this solution was divided equally between two separate beakers, labelled A and B. In beaker A, 1 g of Co2VO4 acid-modified CVA was added, while 1 g of Co2VO4 base NPs (CVB) was added to beaker B. These mixtures were sonicated for 30 min to ensure thorough dispersion of both the glucose and the NPs, promoting better interaction between the materials. After sonication, the contents of both beakers were transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave to undergo hydrothermal treatment. The autoclaves were sealed and heated at 120 °C for 10 h. This hydrothermal process allowed for the integration of CQDs with the Co2VO4 nanomaterials, facilitating the deposition process and enhancing the overall properties of the NCs. Upon completion of the hydrothermal reaction, the resulting NCs were collected and carefully washed with ethanol and deionized water to remove any residual impurities. The washed materials were then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the final products.
As a result of this synthesis process, two distinct CQD deposited NCs were successfully fabricated: Co2VO4 acid/CQDs (CVA/CQDs) and Co2VO4 base/CQDs (CVB/CQDs).

The photocatalytic efficiency of the prepared NCs was evaluated through the degradation kinetic experiment of CIP. The efficacy of the catalyst was obtained by determining the amount of light absorbed by the drug during the process which was accessed by a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photodegradation process of CIP in presence of the prepared photocatalyst CVB/CQDs was done using a visible light source of halogen lamp (500 W). The reaction mixture (20 mL) was prepared with the CIP concentration of 20 mg/L with ultrasonicated catalyst concentration of 50 mg/L, before visible light irradiation the reaction mixture was placed in a shaker maintaining a dark condition for 30 min to attain adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Further, 200 μL of PMS was added to the solution and the reaction mixture was illuminated in visible light to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. Then the UV–Visible absorption spectrum was recorded for every 1 min between the range 200–700 nm until the complete degradation of CIP was obtained. The percentage of degradation was calculated as follows, Eq. (1):
(1)
Degradation % =
A 0
−
A t
A 0
× 100
where A0 is the initial absorbance and At is the absorbance of the drug at time ‘t’. Similarly, the degradation of CIP has also been studied by varying the concentrations of NCs (30–70 mg/L), Drug concentration (10–30 mg/L), pH (3–9) and 1 mg/mL of PMS solution with varying concentrations (50–300 μL). To evaluate the stability and recyclability of the NC, the NCs was filtered and reused after the degradation process for six consecutive cycles which has been further validated by XRD and XPS analysis to study its structural integrity and photo-corrosive nature. To assess the mechanism of photodegradation and to determine the release of free radicals responsible for the catalytic process, the reaction was carried out in the presence of radical trapping agents such as isopropanol (IPA), (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) EDTA, (para benzoquinone) BZQ, (silver nitrate) AgNO3 and methanol for quenching •OH, O2 •−, h+, e− and SO4 •−, respectively.

The structural, elemental and morphology of the fabricated nanomaterials were determined using the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and SAED analysis. Fig. 1a represents the SEM image of pristine NPs where the image clearly displays a well-defined sheet like nano-hexagon. The carbon CQDs were deposited on the surface of prepared Co2VO4 nano-hexagons which resulted in the formation of solid CVB/CQDs (Fig. 1b). The elemental mapping of the NCs exhibits the existence of all individual elements and the elemental composition was determined the presence of Co, V, O and C, respectively. The HR-TEM images of NCs were illustrated in Fig. 1c–e, where the lattice fringes are clearly visible when NPs are viewed in higher magnification to 2 nm scale. The lattice fringes of 0.193 and 0.248 nm corresponds to (220) and (440) planes on CVB/CQDs (Fig. 1d). From the Fig. 1e it is clearly evident that the CVB/CQDs NCs exhibits the mesoporous nature of the NC. These lattice fringes correspond to the characteristic peaks found in the XRD which indicates the crystalline nature of the NCs. The elemental mapping images shows the evenly spread C around the composite, and equal parts of Co and V in the composite representing the purity of the prepared photocatalyst (Fig. 1f–i).

As shown in the Fig. 2 the crystalline structure of the NPs was characterized using XRD. The diffraction peaks at the 2θ of 30.3°, 34.8°, 42.6°, 52.1° and 60° were indexed to the (220), (311), (400), (422) and (440) diffraction planes of the pristine Co2VO4 (JCPDS No.01-073-1633). The tetrahedral and octahedral crystallographic sites of the crystal structure in the NP is occupied by Co and V atoms, respectively, forming a stable crystal structure which has proven to enhance the cyclic stability of the NP [41]. The addition of CQDs to the spinal Co2VO4 resulted in emergence of a distinctive peak at the 2θ of 20.9°. Glucose is used as the carbon source which is used as a precursor for the formation of the NCs CVB/CQDs. Generally, the XRD peak of glucose is observed around 20° [42]. Furthermore, the XRD analysis of CVA and CVA/CQDs were analysed and depicted on Supplementary material Fig. S1.

To determine the chemical bonding states of the CVB/CQDs NCs, the prepared NCs were analysed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The presence of Co, V and O elements are clearly indicated in the full survey of the NCs (Fig. 3a). In the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3b), two major peaks are observed at 779.9 and 795.7 eV which corresponds the existence of Co 2p3/2 and the small peaks at 784.1 and 801.8 eV corresponds to Co 2p1/2, respectively [43]. For the V 2p spectrum, the peaks at 514.4 and 521.4 eV represents the V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2, respectively which is depicted in the Fig. 3c [44]. The O 1 s broad peak can be fitted into three distinctive peaks at 529.6, 530.4 and 531.2 eV, which can be related to the metal – oxygen bonds (M – O), •OH species adsorbed on the surface (M – OH), and low coordination of oxygen ions (OL), respectively [45] (Fig. 3d). The atomic percentage of each compound was determined using the EDX data which is depicted in Supplementary material Fig. S3. The atomic percentage of carbon was found to be 6.92 % of the total volume. The deconvolution of C1s (Fig. 3e) spectrum represented three characteristic peaks at 282.6 eV, 284.1 eV and 286.3 eV which corresponds to the bonded carbon atoms C – C, C – O, and C = O bonds [46–48]. The XPS spectrum of Co2VO4 showed a peak corresponding to Fe, due to the residual minerals from the plant extract [49]. The results also suggest that Fe may also have some considerable influence in the degradation of CIP in the photodegradation process.

The RAMAN spectrum is used to predict the vibrational modes of the NPs and also can be used to determine the chemical structure of the compound and the impurity content of the NPs. The distinctive peaks at 803 and 844.6 cm−1 are attributed to Co–O–V and O–V–O bond vibrations (Fig. 4 ). A distinct change in the peak intensity is observed at 1641.4 cm−1 which may be due to the carbon QDs deposition which resulted in the enhanced degradation efficiency of the NCs CVB/CQDs [50].

To determine the optical properties, the catalyst was characterized using PL. PL is a quantum mechanical process where a photon is absorbed by a material through transferring the energy to an electron, thereby exiting the electron from the ground state to the excited state [51]. Moreover, the photoluminescence spectroscopy is used to determine the dynamic behaviour of the photogenerated e/h+ pairs and it is also used to determine the recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers. Sharp emission peaks are observed at 635.9, 619.1, 585.5, 550.9 and 543.2 nm in both NPs and NCs (Fig. 5a), but the intensity of the NCs peak is much lower compared to the peak intensity of NPs indicating the slow recombination rate of the photogenerated e/h+ pair [52,53]. The reduced recombination of the charge carriers promotes the life time thereby enhancing the photocatalytic ability of the photocatalyst.

The EIS is used to understand the recombination efficiency and charge transfer resistance of the photocatalyst. The EIS Nyquist plot represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the NCs [54]. The smaller the arc in the Nyquist plot corresponds to enhanced charge transfer and longevity and vice versa. The NCs CVB/CQDs exhibits a smaller arc radius which is illustrated in Fig. 5b, whereas the corresponding NPs shows a larger arc radius. This shows that the NCs exhibit a better charge transfer efficiency compared to its counter parts [55].

The efficiency of the photocatalyst is determined by their surface area, surface volume, pore size and atomic arrangement. The increase in the surface properties of the NCs results in the exposure of more active sites of the catalyst which enhances the reactive oxygen species (ROS) production [56,57]. To understand the surface chemistry of NCs, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments were done for the catalyst using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model [58]. The nanomaterials CVB/CQDs NCs and CVB NPs were found to have a surface area of 137.2 and 165.5 m2/g, respectively. The surface volume and the distribution of pore volume values are represented in Supplementary material Table S1. Furthermore, the pore radius of the catalysts was found to be 16.9 and 25.003 Å, respectively. This data indicates the mesoporous nature of the photocatalyst. The mesoporous nature and the increase in the active sites of the NCs resulted in the enhanced interaction of the catalysts, thus improving the degradation efficiency of the NCs against the drug CIP. According to the IUPAC classification and the obtained BET results it is evident that the fabricated NCs CVB/CQDs follows type III H3 hysteresis pattern, where H3 hysteresis pattern is commonly found in materials with significant pore size which plays a major role in photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 5d) [52,59].

The ROS generated by the fabricated CVB/CQDs and the extent of ROS produced is determined using Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis. The ESR results were plotted against the magnetic field and intensity of the signals as shown in the Supplementary material Fig. S2. The ESR signals of the NCs CVB/CQDs displays a strong signal, whereas the NPs shows a week signal. The results of CVB/CQDs correspond to •OH radical which aligns well with the scavenging assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic evaluation and reaction kinetics of CVB/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The efficacy of the NCs was determined through the photocatalysis using CIP as the target pollutant. PMS can produce ROS by the activation of various metal ions to oxidize the pollutants, thereby reducing them to harmless small compounds. Generally, PMS as a typical sulfate oxidant is more chemically stable and it is easy to store, but PMS do not produce any ROS when it is used alone for the degradation of various organic pollutants [60]. The maximum degradation of CIP was achieved within 13 min in the presence of PMS with a degradation percentage of 99.43 %. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model, as presented in Eq. (2),
(2)
− ln
A
A 0
= k × t
where k is the rate constant of the reaction, A0 is the initial absorbance and A is the absorbance at a specific time ‘t’. The kinetic model based on the strong linear correlation between the logarithm of pollutant concentration and reaction time, and the reaction follows pseudo first order. Additionally, in previous studies, Si et al. [61] demonstrated the photocatalytic reaction follows pseudo-first-order kinetics. A similar experiment was performed to evaluate the photocatalytic efficiency of CVA/CQDs, CVA, and CVB in degrading CIP. The results revealed that CVA/CQDs achieved the highest degradation efficiency at 80.75 %, followed by CVB at 76 %, while CVA showed a lower efficiency of 49 %. These findings, depicted in Fig. 6a. The results shows with correlation constants (R2) consistently higher than 0.9 (R2 = 0.9812) implies a strong linear relationship between the experimental data and the fitted model [62]. Furthermore, the regression co-efficient represents a linear and fair regression model of the photocatalytic reaction [63]. According to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model it is evident that the photocatalytic reaction of CIP follows the pseudo-first order reaction which can be attributed to the rate equation, Eq. (3),
(3)
− ln
C /
C t
= kt

From the figure (Fig. 6b) it is observed that the rate of the reaction (k) of CVB/CQDs is 0.37 min−1 which is three times greater than its counterpart CVB (0.10 min−1) indicating the increase in the photocatalytic efficiency of the catalyst upon the incorporation of CQDs to the pristine NP. Additionally, the rate of the reaction of CVA and CVA/CQDs were found to be 0.11 and 0.05 min−1, respectively. Further, the presence of vanadate improves the electrochemical activity of the NCs by enhancing the charge transfer which thereby improves redox reactions. This characteristic facilitates the NCs to function as a bifunctional catalyst to perform as both oxidation and reduction reactions simultaneously [64,65].

The photocatalytic removal of CIP using the fabricated NCs was determined by performing total organic carbon (TOC) analysis. The TOC was estimated using Walkley-black method with a slight modification. The equation for estimating TOC is, Eq. (4)
(4)
TOC
removal
%
=
1 −
TOC
t
TOC
o
× 100
where [TOC]t is the total organic carbon after treatment and [TOC]0 is the total carbon before treatment, respectively. From the analysis, the total TOC after treatment of CIP by using NCs was determined to be 97.81 %. A comparison table was prepared to compare the efficiency of the previous studies involving the degradation of pharmaceutical drugs using PMS activation with the enhanced photodegradation of present study were depicted in the supplementary material Table S2. From which, it can clearly understand that the use of PMS along with the catalyst greatly increases the degradation efficiency. In addition, different nanomaterials used for the degradation of CIP were also studies which is represented in the Supplementary material Table S3. By comparing these studies, we can observe that the prepared NCs exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic degradation of CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various concentration of CIP and catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation process is highly dependent on the concentration of the catalyst, so it is important to study the role of the catalyst concentration by varying the concentration of the NCs CVB/CQDs from 30 to 70 mg/L. The percentage of degradation of the concentrations 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 mg /L after the degradation period of 13 min were 95.5, 96.9, 99.4, 76.8 and 77.2 %, respectively. The results imply that when the concentration of the catalyst increases the degradation efficiency increases, but when the concentration of the catalyst is increased beyond 50 mg/L the efficiency decreases. When the concentration of the catalyst is increased, the solution turns turbid which influences the interaction of the NCs with CIP and also restricts the penetration of the light source into the solution. The results suggest that the photodegradation of CIP is more eminent when the concentration of the catalyst is 50 mg/L (Supplementary material Fig. S4a–b). To determine the effect of the drug concentration, the same procedure was followed by varying the CIP concentration from 10 to 30 mg/L followed by the degradation analysis in 13 min of irradiation. However, it was observed that upon increasing the concentration of the drug only difference in the percentage of degradation were perceived. The degradation percentage of CIP after 13 min of irradiation were 80.8, 93.5, 99.4, 71.01, and 69.08 %, respectively corresponding to the concentrations 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 mg/L (Supplementary material Fig. S4c–d).

In order to determine the effect of PMS concentration in the photocatalytic process, the concentration of PMS was varied between the range 50–300 μL. It was observed that upon increasing the concentration of the PMS, the degradation increases and the optimal concentration was found to be 200 μL. The results suggest that upon increasing the concentration of PMS, the production of ROS also increases which enhances the degradation of CIP [66,67]. However, upon increasing the concentration above 200 μL, the efficiency of the degradation decreased. The above condition is completely related to the concentration of the drug and the catalyst [68,69] (Supplementary material Fig. S5a and b).

The influence of the pH in the degradation of CIP was determined by analysing the degradation efficiency by varying the pH of the solution within the range 3–11 pH. The optimum degradation was observed around pH 5. It can be observed that at extreme acidic and alkaline condition, the efficiency of degradation decreases, and it is also observed that the degradation efficiency is at the lowest when the pH is maintained at 7 (Supplementary material Fig. S5c and d). The reaction was more favourable both in alkaline and acidic condition closer to the neutral condition. The observed trend in photocatalytic degradation efficiency across different pH ranges can be attributed to the surface charge of the catalyst, the ionization state of the pollutant, and the reactive species involved in the degradation process. In the present study, the degradation efficiency increased in the pH range of 3–5, decreased between pH 5–7, and then increased again at pH 8–9 [27]. This trend aligns with previous reports, including Soares et al. [70], who demonstrated efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin at pH 3 and pH 9. The enhancement in acidic and alkaline conditions can be linked to the increased generation of reactive oxidative species, whereas the decline around neutral pH may result from reduced catalyst activity or weaker pollutant-catalyst interactions [70].

Scavenging test were analysed to determine the involvement of the reactive species in the degradation of CIP. In this study, the radical trapping agents/scavengers such as IPA, BQ, EDTA, AgNO3 and methanol for quenching •OH, O2 •−, h+, e− and SO4 •−, respectively. As depicted the addition of IPA exhibited a better degradation performance which represents the active participation of •OH radicals in the degradation of CIP. Moreover, the degradation of CIP significantly dropped after the addition of methanol, which attributes to the dominance of SO4 •− radical. Further, involvement of h+ in degradation process also noted in scavenging study (Supplementary material Fig. S6a). To ensure the reliability of the results, the experiment was conducted in triplicate, and deviations in quenching percentage and degradation efficiency were analysed using standard deviation and standard error. Additionally, ESR analysis was performed to confirm the formation of reactive radicals, as shown in Fig. S2. ESR spectroscopy with DMPO as the spin-trapping agent was utilized, and the ESR graph, plotted against the magnetic field (G) and signal intensity, is presented in the Supplementary material Fig. S2. The enhanced ESR signal observed for NCs indicates a higher production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), particularly •OH radicals, which play a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP.

The reusability efficiency and the structural integrity of the nanomaterial plays a major role in total cost of the large-scale treatment process. In order to determine the stability and reusability the photocatalyst, the particles were subjected six consecutive cycles. Here, NCs was collected after each cycle from the reaction mixture by centrifugation, then, washed and dried and transferred to fresh reaction medium and continued the same for each subsequent cycle. From Fig. 7 , the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the NCs CVB/CQDs after the sixth cycle was found to be 98.5 %, therefore the reusability efficiency was found to be 99.01 %. The reused samples of the NCs were analysed in XRD to determine its structural integrity and reusability, which revealed that there were no significant changes in the XRD pattern of the reused NCs compared to the pristine NCs. From the XRD and reusability (cycles) results it is clearly evident that the NCs is highly stable and possess a remarkable reusability efficiency.

The photocatalytic mechanism of the fabricated CVB/CQDs was proposed based on the results obtained from radical scavenging assays and DRS analysis (Fig. 8 ). Upon visible light irradiation, the NCs absorb the incoming light energy, exciting electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), thereby creating electron-hole pairs. These photogenerated charge carriers play a crucial role in initiating the photocatalytic process by participating in redox reactions [52]. Moreover, the band gap energy of the NCs were determined by using the Kubelka-Munk plot using the results obtained from DRS results (Fig. 5c). Further the CB and VB energy potentials were calculated using the Eqs. (5) and (6).
(5)
E CB
= X −
E c
− 12
E g
(6)
E VB
=
E CB
+
E g
X

The results reveals that the pristine NPs exhibits a wide bandgap which shows a poor catalytic effect against CIP due to the high charge recombination, whereas the NCs CVB/CQDs exhibits a superior photocatalytic performance due to the enhanced separation rate of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs. This recombination potential effectively separates the charge carriers in the NCs [71,72]. The more positive VB potential of CVB/CQDs (EVB = 2.67 eV) compared to -OH/•OH = 2.34 eV vs NHE which favors the h+ to oxidize -OH to generate •OH, however the electrons accumulated in the CB of CVB/CQDs are insufficient to generate O2 •− radicals as it has a low negative potential (ECB = −0.13) compared to O2/O2 •− = −0.33 eV vs NHE [53]. As an effective oxidant, the photogenerated holes can directly interact with CIP. Moreover, XPS analysis confirmed that Co 2p in CVB/CQDs exists in two oxidation states (Co2+ and Co3+) with Co2+ undergoing oxidation to Co3+ by donating an e−. Thus, the produced electron cloud interacts with PMS and can efficiently activate the PMS molecule to produce SO4 •− and •OH radicals [73]. The relevant reactions for the photocatalytic activity of CVB/CQDs for the degradation of CIP are mentioned in the Eqs. (7)–(12).
(7)

In contrast with the conventional photoinactivation process, in PMS activated photocatalytic system, the photogenerated electrons (e−) oxidizes the PMS to form •OH and SO4 •− radicals which actively takes part in the degradation of CIP. Moreover, the oxidation of PMS could also occur by the holes in the VB of the CVB/CQDs to generate SO4 •−, since the oxidation potential of the PMS is 1.8 eV which is much lesser than positive oxidation potential of the NCs [74]. The overall synergistic effect between the photocatalyst and the PMS activation resulted in the generation of abundant active species which enhanced the photocatalytic degradation and mineralization of CIP [75,76].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic pathway for the degradation of CIP is proposed based on the results obtained from GC–MS analysis. The predicted intermediates and the pathway are illustrated in the Fig. 9 with three potential pathways. Due to the activity of the ROS, the parent compound CIP with the m/z value of 332 is anticipated to undergo the predicted degradation pathway. In the photodegradation process of CIP by CVB/CQDs NC, the major active radicals involved in the degradation of CIP are •OH and SO4 •−, in addition, holes (h+) also play a minor role in the degradation process. In this catalytic reaction, •OH being an electrophilic radical and SO4 •− radical being a strong oxidizing agent, it attacks the oxygen atom in CIP and the intermediates and forms H2O. The rigorous attach of the free radical on piperazine ring leads to the formation the intermediates C2 and C3 compounds. The defluorination of C3 compound results in the formation of C4 (m/z = 216) intermediate. The loss of cyclopropyl group in C4 leads to the formation of C8 [77]. The attack of SO4 •− and •OH radicals on C3 (m/z = 306) causes deamination leads to the formation of C5 (m/z = 262) [78]. The oxidation of the piperazine ring in C5 leads to the formation of product C6, where its quinolone group is attacked by the free radicals to form C7. Further, the NH group is oxidized and substituted with carboxylic group which leads to the formation of C9. The complete mineralization of these smaller compounds leads to the formation of final compounds CO2 and H2O.

The toxicity assessment of the intermediates generated during the photocatalytic degradation process was evaluated using ECOSAR program. The acute and chronic toxicity of the antibiotic CIP and its oxidized intermediates were predicted to fish, daphnia and green algae using the results obtained from the ECOSAR software database which is represented in the Fig. 10a and b. The toxicity levels of the intermediates were categorized in four levels namely, very toxic (0–1 mg/L), toxic (>1–10 mg/L), harmful (>10–100 mg/L) and not harmful (>100 mg/L). The results affirm the complete mineralization of CIP by CVB/CQDs, the intermediates and final products formed during the reaction possess lower toxicity than the parent CIP molecule. This ensures that the degradation process does not produce harmful byproducts. These insights underscore the exceptional efficiency of NCs as a promising material for the safe and effective photocatalytic degradation of CIP.

Overall, the fabricated CVB/CQDs NCs showed an exceptional performance (99.3 %) in the photodegradation of CIP. Moreover, PMS activation improved the photocatalytic process by generating SO4 •− radical which plays a key role in the photodegradation of CIP. These radicals initiate •OH formation, accelerated the breakdown of CIP. The successful fabrication of CQDs deposition CVB (CVB/CQDs) was confirmed by various characterizations such as FE-SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, XPS and RAMAN analysis. The surface properties of the NCs were analysed using BET which indicates that the surface area pore size and pore volume of the NCs was comparatively higher than the other materials. The maximum degradation was achieved at the optimal NCs concentration of 50 mg/L. The incorporation of CQDs enhanced the activity of the CVB leads to the complete mineralization of CIP. The intermediates formed during the degradation was analysed by GC–MS analysis. Furthermore, the toxicity of the intermediates was analysed using ECOSAR analysis where the results were categorized as acute and chronic toxicity. The results revealed that the intermediates produced were non-toxic and are harmless to the aquatic environment. Overall, the NCs CVB/CQDs has a great potential in water treatment process which is attributed to its high stability and eco-friendliness.
